subject: RE: report
body:
We can only work as fast as the design product is given to us. The office
package was not available until the end of July and one needs 45 to 60 days
thereafter to line up a contractor and kick off the project, then lead times
apply for materials ( 4 weeks for rock anchors, 12 weeks for structural
steel, 8 weeks for rebar detailing).  If Lafayette made a mistake which
affected timing, it was initial decision to bid the entire package as one
contract asking one entity to perform all work: site, work, rock anchors,
concrete and structural. Breaking out those packages after the initial bids
cost some additional time. On Saturday I sent you a document which, if
implemented, would prevent this kind of delay and speed up the overall pace
of design and construction. I look forward to your comments and questions
about it.  

 

From: J. Epstein [mailto:jeeproject@yahoo.com] 
Sent: Saturday, November 17, 2007 9:03 PM
To: john amerling
Subject: Re: report

 

the question is why are we so far behind. 

----- Original Message ----
From: john amerling <john_amerling@earthlink.net>
To: J. Epstein <jeeproject@yahoo.com>
Sent: Saturday, November 17, 2007 8:37:27 AM
Subject: Re: report

Bill came back late. My note is merely a suggestion that a number based upon
the actual design which is due out next week would be far more beneficial to
you. I may be able track down Bill today to obtain from him the preliminary
unit price based information which in his computer files. I continue to
believe that the many changes between the conceptual drawings which we had
and the building plans which are to receive will yeild a different and
better number. The information we now have is very, very preliminary and may
be very misleading. I will try to locate Bill if you wish. I just do not
want to have you mislead positively or negatively. Please let me know what
you think. 

On the second topic  discussed yesterday, the answer to the management
issues was already in my computer in the form of a comprehensive management
which I put together in September and presented in New York on September 19
because I thought that the process then being followed was inefficient and
too expensive. If implemented it would provide sufficient authority and
local autonomy to make the project move to your satisfaction. 

I have a couple of things I have to do today. I will try to monitor the
emails every few hours. 

-----Original Message----- 
From: "J. Epstein" 
Sent: Nov 17, 2007 5:33 AM 
To: John Amerling 
Subject: Re: report 

you told me in no uncertain terms that ther was a preliminary budget, of
between 5-6 million dollars,,, I asked for the detail, you told me bill had
it , and would be back shortly, he came back before 4 o clock. I asked you
to e- mail me.. This is not what you promised.

body_html:
We can only work as fast as the design product is given to us. The office package was not available until the end of July and one needs 45 to 60 days thereafter to line up a contractor and kick off the project, then lead times apply for materials ( 4 weeks for rock anchors, 12 weeks for structural steel, 8 weeks for rebar detailing).  If Lafayette made a mistake which affected timing, it was initial decision to bid the entire package as one contract asking one entity to perform all work: site, work, rock anchors, concrete and structural. Breaking out those packages after the initial bids cost some additional time. On Saturday I sent you a document which, if implemented, would prevent this kind of delay and speed up the overall pace of design and construction. I look forward to your comments and questions about it.

Bill came back late. My note is merely a suggestion that a number based upon the actual design which is due out next week would be far more beneficial to you. I may be able track down Bill today to obtain from him the preliminary unit price based information which in his computer files. I continue to believe that the many changes between the conceptual drawings which we had and the building plans which are to receive will yeild a different and better number. The information we now have is very, very preliminary and may be very misleading. I will try to locate Bill if you wish. I just do not want to have you mislead positively or negatively. Please let me know what you think.

On the second topic  discussed yesterday, the answer to the management issues was already in my computer in the form of a comprehensive management which I put together in September and presented in New York on September 19 because I thought that the process then being followed was inefficient and too expensive. If implemented it would provide sufficient authority and local autonomy to make the project move to your satisfaction. 

I have a couple of things I have to do today. I will try to monitor the emails every few hours.
